sentences.text: It was very hard, for sure.
We couldn't sit.
We shouldn't.
We didn't work.
We had to stay by the walls.
We could sit then, when they brought us the food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is it.
16 and 3 months.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nothing special was happen.
Just in the time that we were there, we had four ladies more.
They took out two-- three ladies, two sisters and a-- and she was, I think, the wife of the brother.
I don't know for sure.
They took them out.
They say they take them to the ghetto.
Three days later, they brought their coats back to us.
So we understood that they got shot.
And then they took out one.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they said they took her to the ghetto.
And they did.
Ola Kusznier, she was alive.
When I came, I found her in the ghetto.
The Germans, who were-- in everything that did go on, when the Germans were, the Germans.
There could be-- they called them kalfaktors, the people who clean, who-- who help to give food.
So they don't have to pay for the work that people have to do.
So the people who think like we-- us, they were the kalfaktors.
They were the people who did everything in sight-- and dig graves.
One Sunday, in the beginning of-- of April, we heard, in the middle of the night, yelling.
We had three backyards.
This was the third back yard.
They killed 250 nuns.
They had to make their own graves, because they were nun and Polish.
And 250 chrzescijanin.
Chrzescijanin is like here a-- not a preacher, a-- a-- like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, who is-- oh God.
Failed me, the word.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
A minister?
Is more than a minister.
Is the person who doesn't wear, here, a piece of white.
He wears all around white and a black man-dress.
He can't have a wife.
He doesn't have to do with ladies.
Yes.
They Christians.
And they were killed, 500 people in one night.
We heard this.
And then we have seen the graves.
And later on, what they did, they send the Jewish people to take them out, burn the-- the bones, and put it back, and straighten it out so nobody will know.
Then, by the end of the ghetto, they did it with the Jewish people in Ponary.
They took 38 people.
They let them stay in Ponary.
And they had to kill-- to take out-- this is somebody working-- to take out the bones, burn them.
And they put it like this, like it's-- it's sugar.
And they put it back.
And they straighten it out, so people will not find the bones.
This was when I was in ghetto.
And I worked for a-- for the soldiers-- not the SS, plain soldiers.
We cleaned their-- we washed the toilets, the-- the-- the steps, everything.
They didn't pay us.
And we had to work.
After I was released from jail, I came back to the ghetto.
I was, from the 19, first girls who came back, officially, through Officer Weiss.
He allowed us to go back.
SS.
SS, yes.
He was SS.
And--
In the end-- half May.
And--
In the same place.
I couldn't go back, because I didn't know no nobody.
I had to go where I was.
My father was then in ghetto.
He was working, painting for the ghetto.
They need for the-- we had a Jewish theater.
They need [NON-ENGLISH], all kind of stuff.
He make them.
We had a-- first of all, we had a library, Strashun's Library.
We had a theater.
We had a cafe.
We had, by us, was Advocat Muszkat.
He maked a special school for children, who the-- were alive and their parents were killed.
He took them in, with sleeping, with everything.
He teach them like in school.
And they-- and he makes so that he get the food.
Advocat Muszkat.
Then, what did we have more?
We had partisan, too.
Yeah.
And our ghetto Jewish commandant, inside, helped them to go out.
In-- What was his name?
Jacob Gens.
He helped them every night.
He would let out, through a second door, 200 people.
No, no, no, no.
No, we had the police, too.
No.
He wasn't chief of police.
He was, in Jewish, they called him the Ghettovorsteher.
He was the person who talked for every Jew in the ghetto.
But he couldn't do nothing.
No.
The Judenrat-- the Judenrat were separate.
Where my father worked, there was the Judenrat.
And in the other side, it was the police.
The first station police.
We had four stations police.
He was-- he was like a-- well, we say here, is in-- in-- in-- in this town, it's a small town, they have a-- a-- how do you call them?
A mayor?
A mayor of the town.
He was the mayor of the-- of the ghetto.
But he had to talk to the SS and tell them what he thinks, how it goes.
No, he didn't tell them the truth, that's for sure.
He was very intelligent.
He was a maior.
A maior is a major in the army.
And everybody loved him.
So they believed him.
And this was very much for the Jewish people.
He didn't save nobody.
He just sent the people who belong to the-- to the partisans, he sent them out, so they can save themselves.
He didn't take money for it, the opposite.
He for them-- for some-- he got guns, with-- because he-- I could go.
I couldn't, because I didn't have a gun.
You had to have a gun, because the partisan couldn't give you a gun.
Three of my girlfriends, they did go.
Through Christian people.
You know, when you work, you are working for Christian people or for the Germans.
And you-- everybody asks, do you have what to sell?
They give you bread.
You give them a coat.
It's-- so through the people.
Oh yes.
We worked.
I-- I worked for the luftpark.
Luftpark is like here-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] like Newark Airport, like-- like Kennedy Airport.
It was the first soldier's airport.
We didn't have before an airport special for soldiers.
In Vilna.
But it was, from the city, 15 kilometers.
We had to walk back and forth.
I worked.
And we worked.
All-- all of us worked.
We rake the-- the-- the ground.
We took-- put the wood.
They make the places in case somebody will bomb, they will have where to be-- not us, them.
So this was what I did in ghetto.
Then, they sent me to Christian farmers to work.
So the Christian farmers at least gave me to eat-- not me, everybody.
We worked, 150 people.
What kind of work did you do there?
Everything.
Not their thing-- I-- I mean, not to clean houses-- outside, outside.
Put potatoes, take out potatoes, clean between-- you know, there is a grass that's very terrible for your feet when you-- you get it.
And then--
Oh, yeah.
Yeah, very good soup.
Polish farmers, they feed us.
And--
Of course they asked.
They asked, and we tell them the truth.
And they helped.
They-- the Polish peopled helped.
They took away-- when they tooked people to Ponary to shot them, and people-- ladies had small children, they tooked away the children from them.
They can get-- they couldn't get shot the same way that us.
The Polish people, and they hold them by them.
Yes.
That's the--
Of course.
But there was-- and Ponary was written, for Jewish people, for Polish people, and plain soldiers, not to go inside.
Ponary was a place where they took people, 10 in one side, 10 in the other, with machine guns, and they would shot them, and they will fall in.
But before they took them, you had to get completely naked.
In the beginning-- oy, I have to tell him, too.
In the beginning, they closed their eyes.
But people didn't want to.
They didn't want to.
They said, we want to get through that and see what's going on.
How do I know for sure?
I know for sure, because one of the girls, from the first 20,000, 28,000 people, came back.
Yeah.
She was the last 10.
Her mother, her sister, the sister's children, I don't know how many.
They were 10 people.
They had to stay like this, with each other.
And she was the last one.
And she waked up.
And she got out.
I don't know how.
And a Christian, in the morning, 4 o'clock in the morning, found her, and he took her to his house.
And he hold her there two weeks.
Her name was [NON-ENGLISH].
Her second name, I don't know.
But she was--
Yes.
She was-- she had been shot here, [POINTING] here, through here.
But she was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not full with her mind.
But she told us what happened to her.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was Ponary.
They didn't make the decision who would go.
They took like-- like herrings.
One, they came in the ghetto.
They made a action.
They called it aktion.
They took three days.
The first one was two days before Yom Kippur, the first one.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took 6,000 people, not more.
The second one was at least 15,000 people, 2 days.
They didn't select.
They select in concentration camp, to send in concentration camp.
But they didn't select when they took the people from ghetto.
They took them.
They come in, in a-- in a house, in an apartment.
People, everybody out.
Downstairs, everybody.
So the people did go.
And then they took them, with the-- with the trucks, to-- to Ponary.
They did know where they were going.
But they didn't do-- what could they do?
They didn't have guns.
They didn't have to-- where to go.
They didn't have money.
They didn't have nothing.
So they go where they took them.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
They took 4,000 young people one day in '30-- in '43, in the beginning of May.
The young people weren't from our city.
They bought them from another city.
And they took them to Ponary to get shot.
And some of them, with their feet, took out wood from the train, and ran and came back to the ghetto.
And they telled us what they did.
They shot them in the trains.
And then they put them, like-- like junk, in the graves.
Always.
Every street-- we had 14 streets.
That's all.
Every street could, any time, go to the aktion, go to the-- to Ponary.
They took away around-- almost 50,000 people.
They left 24,000 people.
But they didn't know that we are 40-- 24,000.
They did think 10, 12, because the people who didn't work, they didn't walk in the streets. 7 o'clock was dead.
You couldn't walk out the street, because you would be-- get shot from the SS around the ghetto.
So they sit home.
And we brought them food.
Some had, some didn't.
My father had a working paper.
So I had a girlfriend-- there were three sisters and a mother.
And I asked my father to take them on his papers.
Not more than five.
They are not stupid, you know?
And they know that the Vilna people didn't have many children, because there was not money.
So my father took them.
There was Selah, Zhenya, and Hoda, three sisters.
They died in concentration camp, in Struthof.
If-- if you take them on your paper, yes, as long as there wasn't a second aktion.
By the set-- second action, you had to have new papers.
You had white papers, rosa papers, yellow papers, and passports.
The last was the passport.
So by the passport, they took the last-- Kittel, General Kittel closed the ghetto, taking 24,000 people.
From the 24,000, he took 2,000 to the concentration camp and 22,000 to Majdanek.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was between the 2,000.
Before we get to that, tell me a little bit more about the ghetto.
When there was an aktion, and it involved your street, what did you do?
[LAUGHS] Well, I got-- got upstairs, in the attic, in a cellar, where it was a store, once, but it wasn't anymore.
I was in a-- we had a house.
The house was three-- three stories.
In the kitchen, we opened a hole.
And we were there, 18 people, 3 days and nights.
In a hole.
But this was under the-- under the ceiling-- under the-- oh, God.
[LAUGHS] It was closed, but not so closed that the water didn't get through.
And these three days, it was raining.
So we had the water on our heads, everybody.
In between us was a man, who they brought him just from the hospital.
He had amputate the right foot.
And the-- the SS was looking where they can find people, because when they walked in, nobody was there.
He faengt an to cry from pain.
So somebody closed his mouth with a coat.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the hole where we were was a small-- a small closet.
And they didn't think that under the closet are people.
So they left us be.
To every apartment.
Yes, of course.
We heard them talking.
We heard them saying what they said, everything.
And then they go out, away. 5 o'clock, they-- they-- they had 3 days, up to 5 o'clock.
From 7:00 in the morning, people who had the-- the scheins, the-- the paper, did go to the-- to the people, to the SS.
And they said, you out, you stay.
You out.
You stay.
The schein was the paper.
Schein was the paper.
Yes, yes.
And--
Yeah.
No, no, no.
No food.
We didn't, because we runned.
We didn't think about it.
And I-- I-- I think there wasn't where to put the food.
What I remember is we had a lady called Berta.
She was a very tall lady.
I've never seen as a-- So your feet would come to the other wall.
And here, it would be up.
And the sick man would say, [LAUGHS] do something to your feet.
Because I can't stand.
I have to sleep, she said.
What should I do?
Eat half of them?
[LAUGHS] And then, where I was lying, all the time I had, on my right side, water.
This is-- this is a terrible feeling to have.
When you hear that the water triff, you feel terrible.
But then when it goes on your head?
So-- And you couldn't move?
No.
If we had to move, we had to move everybody, because one couldn't.
And there wasn't enough place.
In this hiding, no.
We had, in another hiding, was a child, Szymon, five years old, and a child, nine months old.
This was two days aktion.
And the child faengt an to cry.
So Szymon said to his mother, mother, why don't you close the child's mouth?
She says, he will die.
He said, OK, he's 1 and we are 85.
And they put a pillow over him, and he died.
There were 85 of you?
Yes, in the--
This was in the ghetto, on the street called Strashuna, 16-- 15, on the 2nd floor.
We were in two rooms, in a small hall.
And there was a china.
And they had to open the china in last moment.
And the china opened, but there was vodka.
[LAUGHS] So they fang an to drink the vodka.
They got drunk.
They forgot about us.
And they had to go out.
For-- of course!
They came in every apartment, in every place where there was a possibility that there are people, to take them to kill them.
Yeah.
Not first, by the end.
By the last, third day, they open-- they want to-- to take away the-- the closet, the china, and see what's inside.
So they opened, and one said, 'Wir haben Schnapps,' they had vodka.
So they fang an to drink the vodka.
And thank to this, we stayed alive.
And what I remember is that my friend's mother, came the first one, with her two daughters and son by the hand, and she said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my child, where is Maria, where am I?
He said, she's behind me.
She said, God should help you, you should always have light, that you have taken the child with you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I will never forget it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the ghetto.
No.
The last action, we couldn't hide, because they said, this is-- this is the liquidation from the ghetto.
This was September 22, in '43.
Well three, but one-- the largest was the third one.
At all?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 62, 63, something like that.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And the last one, it was 86,000 Jewish people.
But they brought people from around the city, from little towns, from little, I don't know, places.
Because they couldn't do it there.
So they brought them to the ghetto and, from the ghetto, to Ponary, and they shot them there.
Everybody who came got shot.
And then-- [SIGHS] then they said that we are closing the ghetto.
We're sending you to concentration camp Kaiserwald.
Where was your father all of this time?
[MOAN] My father was taken, two months before, from the Germans.
They send him to Estonia.
This is in the Baltic, too.
It's the first-- the third Baltic state.
To Estonia, to a-- a small town called Klooga.
There were 6,000 people.

And four hours before the Russians came in to free the-- the concentration camp, my father and the rest-- just 103 people, run away, because they didn't have time to run after them-- were burned in the street by the concentration camp.
The pictures is in every Jewish museum.
How is it, when they-- when they run away, the Germans?
My cousin's husband did go to the post in a coat, and he asked for the International Red Cross.
And they make the pictures.
In between the people, there is my father.
So he was killed as part of a mass group?
Burned, yes.
I find out a little bit later than my girlfriends, because I was-- I-- it's too-- [LAUGHS] We didn't talk yet about the concentration camp.
This was after the concentration camp.
Yeah.
They're making the final aktion.
They took us to where there was nuns, a place where the nuns had the biggest place in Vilna, in-- in the backyard.
We sat there, 24,000 people, 400 children.
And they took us for selection.
The young people, left-- right, and the older people, left.
So I didn't want to go.
I couldn't take it anymore.
So the Kittel, the general, said to me, you are young.
You go to work.
And he sent me left-- right.
I-- yes.
I couldn't.
I was finished with my nerves.
But he sent me.
And I did go.
What could I do?
I have see a terrible-- two terrible tragedies when they-- they tooked us.
First of all, a Polish Christian lady came to the people who stood there, and she did go straight to a man.
And she said, here, you have the package.
And I'm going with you.
So the Kittel come and said, who are you?
She said, I am his wife.
He said, go away.
He is going to be shot.
She said, OK, so I'm going to be shot.
He said, you don't have to wait long.
He took them both to the wall, everybody of us have seen, and shot them both.
This was the one.
And now you have here the picture I showed you, from my girlfriend, who passed away this year.
OK.
